prompt:
Passage 1
A regional warehouse recently updated its process for checking outgoing orders. Previously, employees performed a final count by comparing the packed boxes with a printed order sheet. This method worked most of the time, but errors sometimes occurred when sheets were misplaced or when similar products had nearly identical names.
The warehouse now uses a scanning step before an order is released for shipping. Employees scan each packed item and compare it with the order record in the system. If the wrong item or quantity is scanned, the system alerts the employee before the order leaves the packing area.
Managers report that the scanning step has reduced shipping errors and helped employees catch mistakes earlier. The system does not replace careful work, but it adds another check before the customer receives the order.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The warehouse stopped checking outgoing orders.
choices.B: A scanning step improved accuracy in outgoing order checks.
choices.C: Printed order sheets are always more reliable than scanning.
choices.D: Customers now scan their own orders before shipment.

explanation: The passage explains that scanning outgoing items helps employees catch wrong items or quantities before shipping.

prompt:
Passage 2
NOTICE
Beginning August 5, all employees using the shared loading dock must record trailer arrival and departure times in the dock log. Employees should also note whether the trailer was loaded, unloaded, or waiting for paperwork.
The updated log procedure was created after several trailers waited longer than expected without a clear record of the reason. This made it difficult for supervisors to identify whether delays were caused by staffing, paperwork, equipment availability, or scheduling.
The dock lead will review the log at the end of each day and discuss repeated delays during the weekly operations meeting.

What is the purpose of the notice?

choices.A: To announce a new procedure for recording trailer activity at the loading dock
choices.B: To close the loading dock permanently
choices.C: To remove trailers from the facility
choices.D: To cancel weekly operations meetings

explanation: The notice announces the new dock log procedure for recording trailer arrivals, departures, and status.

prompt:
Passage 3
Many companies use written approval limits to guide purchasing decisions. An approval limit states how much money an employee or manager is allowed to spend without additional permission. For example, a supervisor may be allowed to approve small supply orders, while larger equipment purchases require review by a department manager.
Approval limits help organizations control spending and reduce confusion. Employees know who can approve different types of purchases, and managers can focus on higher-cost decisions. These limits also create a record of responsibility when questions arise about why money was spent.
However, approval limits must be communicated clearly. If employees do not understand the limits, they may delay necessary purchases or place orders without proper review.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Approval limits help guide purchasing decisions when employees understand them.
choices.B: Employees should make all purchases without review.
choices.C: Approval limits are used only for customer refunds.
choices.D: Large purchases should never be reviewed.

explanation: The passage explains that approval limits guide purchasing decisions and work only when employees understand them.

prompt:
Passage 4
Before digital maps became common, drivers often relied on printed maps, written directions, or local knowledge to reach unfamiliar places. These tools were useful, but they could be difficult to update when roads changed, exits closed, or new routes became available.
Digital mapping systems changed navigation by providing updated route information, estimated travel times, and turn-by-turn directions. Many systems can also show traffic conditions, construction areas, or alternate routes. This helps drivers adjust plans when conditions change.
Digital maps are not perfect. A driver may still need to watch road signs, confirm addresses, and use judgment when a suggested route seems unsafe or unsuitable. The technology is most helpful when used along with careful attention to actual road conditions.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Printed maps are always more accurate than digital maps.
choices.B: Digital maps improved navigation but still require driver judgment.
choices.C: Drivers should ignore road signs when using digital maps.
choices.D: Digital maps are useful only when roads never change.

explanation: The passage explains that digital maps improved navigation but still require drivers to use judgment and watch real conditions.

prompt:
Passage 5
A regional office recently introduced a visitor appointment system. Previously, visitors arrived at the front desk and waited while staff contacted the person they came to see. If the employee was in a meeting or away from the building, the visitor sometimes had to wait or reschedule.
With the new system, employees enter visitor appointments in advance. The entry includes the visitor’s name, arrival time, meeting location, host employee, and whether a badge is required. Front desk staff can review the schedule at the start of each day and prepare badges before visitors arrive.
The office reports that visitor check-in is faster and that front desk staff can better manage busy periods. The system also helps employees confirm that they are available before inviting visitors to the building.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Visitors are no longer allowed in the office.
choices.B: A visitor appointment system made check-in more organized and efficient.
choices.C: Front desk staff stopped preparing badges.
choices.D: Employees now avoid scheduling meetings.

explanation: The passage explains how the visitor appointment system made visitor check-in faster and more organized.

prompt:
Passage 6
Many workplaces use housekeeping inspections to keep work areas clean and organized. These inspections may check whether walkways are clear, tools are stored properly, trash is removed, and materials are stacked safely.
Housekeeping inspections are not only about appearance. A cluttered area can create tripping hazards, block emergency exits, or make it harder to find needed tools. Regular inspections can help identify problems before they lead to injuries or lost time.
Inspections are most effective when results are acted on. If the same blocked walkway appears on several inspection reports, supervisors may need to change storage practices or assign clearer cleanup responsibilities.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Housekeeping inspections support safety and organization when followed by action.
choices.B: Housekeeping inspections are only about making work areas look nice.
choices.C: Cluttered areas make tools easier to find.
choices.D: Inspection reports should be ignored.

explanation: The passage explains that housekeeping inspections support safety and organization, especially when findings lead to action.

prompt:
Passage 7
Many communities use public lighting in parking lots, sidewalks, and transit stops to improve visibility after dark. Good lighting can help people see walking surfaces, signs, vehicles, and other people nearby.
Lighting design requires balance. Too little light may make an area difficult to use safely. Too much poorly directed light can create glare or disturb nearby homes. Communities may choose fixtures that direct light downward and use timers or sensors to control when lights operate.
Public lighting also requires maintenance. Burned-out bulbs, damaged poles, or blocked fixtures can reduce visibility. Regular checks help keep lighting systems useful and reliable.

What is the passage mainly about?

choices.A: Public lighting improves visibility when designed and maintained properly.
choices.B: Communities should remove all lighting from public areas.
choices.C: Bright glare is always the goal of lighting design.
choices.D: Public lighting never needs maintenance.

explanation: The passage explains that public lighting improves visibility when it is well designed and maintained.

prompt:
Passage 8
An apprenticeship preparation program recently added short “mixed review” sessions to its final month of classes. Earlier lessons focused on one skill at a time, such as main idea, vocabulary, equations, or ratios. Instructors noticed that students performed well during single-skill practice but sometimes struggled when different question types appeared together.
During mixed review, students complete short sets that combine several skills. Afterward, instructors discuss how to recognize what each question is asking before choosing a method. The goal is to help students shift between skills more smoothly during the entrance exam.
Students report that mixed review feels more challenging than single-skill practice, but many say it better resembles the experience of a real test.

Why did the program add mixed review sessions?

choices.A: Students sometimes struggled when different question types appeared together.
choices.B: Students no longer needed final-month classes.
choices.D: Single-skill practice was never used.

explanation: Mixed review was added because students struggled when different question types appeared together.

prompt:
Passage 8
An apprenticeship preparation program recently added short “mixed review” sessions to its final month of classes. Earlier lessons focused on one skill at a time, such as main idea, vocabulary, equations, or ratios. Instructors noticed that students performed well during single-skill practice but sometimes struggled when different question types appeared together.
During mixed review, students complete short sets that combine several skills. Afterward, instructors discuss how to recognize what each question is asking before choosing a method. The goal is to help students shift between skills more smoothly during the entrance exam.
Students report that mixed review feels more challenging than single-skill practice, but many say it better resembles the experience of a real test.

Which statement is best supported by the passage?

choices.A: Mixed review can help students prepare for the variety of a real exam.
choices.B: Single-skill practice and real tests are always identical.
choices.C: Students should avoid practicing multiple skills together.
choices.D: Mixed review makes it impossible to recognize question types.

explanation: The passage supports the idea that mixed review helps students prepare for the variety of a real entrance exam.
